file_name: Shukla et al. - 2024 - A hybrid CNN with transfer learning for skin cancer disease detection.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes and evaluates a hybrid Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with transfer learning combined with a Random Forest classifier for the task of skin cancer disease detection (classifying benign vs. malignant skin lesions). The model achieved an accuracy of 90.11% on publicly available skin cancer datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL ARTICLE' and presents a novel hybrid CNN with transfer learning model for skin cancer detection. It details original methodology, experiments on public datasets (D1 and D2), and reports new empirical results (e.g., 90.11% accuracy), rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: A hybrid CNN with transfer learning for skin cancer disease detection
brief_summary: This study proposes a hybrid Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with transfer learning combined with a Random Forest classifier to detect and classify skin cancer as either benign or malignant. The model was evaluated on two publicly available datasets: a smaller dataset (D1) of 1,800 images and a larger combined dataset (D2) of 13,902 images sourced from Kaggle and the ISIC collaboration. The methodology involved resizing images to 224x224 pixels, converting color spaces, and splitting the data into 80% for training and 20% for testing. The proposed HCTL model achieved a classification accuracy of 90.11% when using EfficientNetV2B3 for feature extraction. The empirical results demonstrate the feasibility and effectiveness of this hybrid approach for accurate skin cancer disease detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: Classified as malignant in the binary classification task. The paper notes that malignant skin cancer is also known as melanoma. Dataset D2 contains 6,602 images of malignant skin cancer.

Classified as benign in the binary classification task. The paper defines benign skin cancer as lesions that are ordinarily less aggressive and do not metastasize, such as basal cell carcinoma and squamous cell carcinoma. Dataset D2 contains 7,300 images of benign skin cancer.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into malignant or benign categories
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'skin cancer disease detection' and 'classification of skin lesions' into 'malignant' or 'benign' classes. The model uses a hybrid CNN with transfer learning and a Random Forest classifier to predict discrete diagnostic labels (benign vs. malignant). Performance is evaluated using accuracy, recall, F1 score, and precision on a binary classification task.

datasets_used.name: D1 (Malignant vs. Benign)
datasets_used.description: A smaller dataset consisting of images of benign and malignant skin moles, sourced from Kaggle.

use.training: 80% of images (1440) used for training the hybrid CNN with transfer learning model.
use.testing: 20% of images (360) used for testing the model.

datasets_used.name: D2 (Combined Dataset)
datasets_used.description: A larger combined dataset sourced from multiple sources including Kaggle and the ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collaboration). It aggregates three specific datasets: 'Malignant vs. benign' (Kaggle), 'Melanoma cancer dataset' (Kaggle), and 'Skin cancer ISIC' (Kaggle/ISIC).

use.training: 80% of images (11,121.6) used for training the hybrid CNN with transfer learning model.
use.testing: 20% of images (2,780.4) used for testing the model.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using empirical metrics such as accuracy, recall, F1 score, and precision on public datasets.

D1 (Malignant vs. Benign).description: Dataset of 1,800 images (80% training, 20% testing). Evaluated with various hybrid CNN models combined with Random Forest Classifier (RFC).

HCTL (VGG16 + RFC).notes: Performance improved by 0.89-0.43% over original VGG-16 (58.6%) but specific D1 value not explicitly stated as a single number in text, though Fig 14 shows it is lower than EfficientNetV2B3.

HCTL (VGG19 + RFC).notes: Performance is 'a little bit less' than other models but 'more than 80%'.

HCTL (ResNet50 + RFC).notes: Described as having the 'best performance of classification' in terms of accuracy and computational power on D1, but exact percentage not explicitly isolated in text compared to D2.

HCTL (DenseNet121 + RFC).notes: Performance improved by 0.29-0.34% over original VGG-16 and VGG-19.

HCTL (Xception + RFC).notes: Performance consistently improves over single datasets.

HCTL (EfficientNetB3 + RFC).notes: Performed poorly on D1 compared to other models.

D1 (Malignant vs. Benign).notes: The 80:20 validation/testing ratio was found to be appropriate for testing and validation. Confusion matrix data for DenseNet-121 on D2 is provided (1281 true malignant, 1151 true benign, 168 false malignant, 181 false benign), but specific D1 confusion counts are not detailed in the text.

D2 (Combined Dataset).description: Dataset of 13,902 images (80% training, 20% testing). Aggregated from Kaggle and ISIC sources. Evaluated with various hybrid CNN models combined with Random Forest Classifier (RFC).

HCTL (EfficientNetV2B3 + RFC).notes: Attained the top accuracy over D2. Recall value is significantly high, up to 91%.

HCTL (VGG16 + RFC).notes: Performance consistently grows over large hybrid dataset.

HCTL (VGG19 + RFC).notes: Performance consistently improves when used with mixed dataset.

HCTL (ResNet50 + RFC).notes: F1-score reduced by 1.0% from D1 dataset over mixed large datasets.

HCTL (EfficientNetB3 + RFC).notes: Accuracy and recall over D2 are only 1% more than D1.

D2 (Combined Dataset).notes: The model performed better in HSV format (90.11% max accuracy) compared to RGB format (89.06% max accuracy). Confusion matrix data for DenseNet-121 on D2: 1281 true malignant, 1151 true benign, 168 false malignant, 181 false benign. Confusion matrix data for EfficientNetB3 on D2: 1159 true benign, 1011 true malignant, 155 false malignant, 124 false benign. Confusion matrix data for VGG16 on D2: 1316 true benign, 1175 true malignant, 165 false malignant, 125 false benign.

Comparative Baselines (External Studies).description: Results from other studies cited for comparison.
Comparative Baselines (External Studies).notes: The proposed HCTL model (90.11% on D2) significantly outperforms these open standard skin cancer ISIC dataset and hybrid dataset results.

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid CNN with Transfer Learning (HCTL) combined with a Random Forest Classifier (RFC). The CNN backbone utilizes multiple pre-trained architectures: DenseNet121, EfficientNetB3, EfficientNetV2B3, ResNet50, VGG16, VGG19, and Xception.
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained weights from the ImageNet dataset were used for the CNN backbones. The classifier heads (flattening and dense layers) were excluded during loading.

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Features were extracted from the pre-trained CNN models excluding the classifier head.

classifier_parameters.max_features: square root of the total features

architecture_details.activation_function: Softmax (used in the fully connected two neurons in the last layer of binary classification for the CNN base models)

training_details.hardware: Ryzen 9 6900 HX processor, 16 GB physical memory, NVIDIA RTX 3070ti with 8 GB VRAM
training_details.epochs: 100 epochs (for VGG16 optimization mentioned as baseline; HCTL models were loaded with pre-trained weights and optimized for skin cancer lesions)
training_details.color_space: HSV (Hue Saturation Value) images were used for the primary evaluation, though RGB was also tested.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: HCTL (Hybrid CNN with Transfer Learning) combined with Random Forest Classifier (RFC)
DenseNet 201 & MobNet with SVM (Baseline from Study [43])
DenseNet 201 & ResNet with SVM (Baseline from Study [43])
CNN with novel regularizer (Baseline from Study [20])
preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to 256 × 256 pixels initially, then to 224 × 224 pixels for model input
Color space conversion from RGB to BGR
Color space conversion from BGR to HSV (Hue, Saturation, Value)
Dataset balancing by selecting specific counts of benign (7300) and malignant (6602) images
Data splitting into 80% training and 20% testing sets
conclusions: The proposed hybrid CNN with transfer learning (HCTL) combined with a Random Forest Classifier (RFC) is feasible and effective for skin cancer disease detection.
The HCTL model utilizing EfficientNetV2B3 for feature extraction achieved a classification accuracy of 90.11% on the larger combined dataset (D2) and 89.26% on the smaller dataset (D1).
The proposed HCTL model significantly outperforms existing open standard skin cancer ISIC dataset results and other hybrid dataset approaches cited in the literature.
Using HSV color space images resulted in higher accuracy (up to 90.11%) compared to RGB format images (up to 89.06%).
An 80:20 training-to-testing data split ratio was determined to be optimal for model performance compared to other ratios (50:50, 60:40, 70:30, 90:10).
Transfer learning allows for the creation of highly accurate models with less labeled data, addressing the challenge of acquiring large amounts of high-quality labeled dermatological data.
The model demonstrates consistent performance improvements when applied to both single datasets and combined large hybrid datasets of benign and malignant skin lesions.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on publicly available datasets (Kaggle and ISIC) and lacks external validation on independent clinical cohorts, limiting generalizability to real-world clinical settings.

The dataset D2 was artificially balanced by selecting specific counts of benign (7,300) and malignant (6,602) images, which may not reflect the true prevalence distribution of skin lesions in clinical practice.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals were reported; performance was evaluated solely using empirical metrics (accuracy, recall, F1 score, precision).
The paper does not report data leakage risks or detailed cross-validation strategies beyond a single 80:20 train-test split, raising concerns about the robustness of the reported 90.11% accuracy.

The model was evaluated using HSV color space images, which yielded higher accuracy (90.11%) compared to RGB (89.06%), but the clinical relevance of this preprocessing choice for standard dermatological imaging workflows is not discussed.

The study compares its results against baselines from other papers (e.g., Study [20] with 97.47% accuracy) but does not re-run these baselines on the same datasets, making direct comparative validity difficult to assess.

The hardware specifications (Ryzen 9, RTX 3070ti) and framework details are provided, but computational efficiency metrics (e.g., inference time, FLOPs) are not reported, limiting assessment of deployment feasibility.

The paper acknowledges the challenge of acquiring large amounts of high-quality labeled dermatological data but does not address potential biases inherent in the public datasets used (e.g., demographic bias in image sources).
